The CLI works because it has these environment variables set in the shell:\n- `BEDROCK_BASE_URL=bedrock.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com`\n- `ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_SONNET_MODEL=arn:aws:bedrock:eu-west-1:107001693847:application-inference-profile/j9ve0scyk094`\n- `ANTHROPIC_MODEL=arn:aws:bedrock:eu-west-1:107001693847:application-inference-profile/j9ve0scyk094`\n- `AWS_PROFILE=ai-devtools-dev`\n\n\n- `AWS_CA_BUNDLE=C:\\Users\\Ukkepdo\\proxy_cert\\blsg_leumi_2029.pem`\n\nThe SSO token file is named `ai-helper-sso-token.json` rather than following the standard AWS CLI naming convention, which suggests a custom credentials provider is being used. The CLI works fine while `aws sts get-caller-identity --profile ai-devtools-dev` fails with \"Token for leumi does not exist\", so the CLI might be bypassing the standard AWS credential chain and using `BEDROCK_BASE_URL` directly.\n\nLooking back at the credentials file, the `ai-devtools-dev` profile actually contains static temporary credentials—an access key, secret key, and session token—rather than relying solely on SSO. This means the AWS SDK credential resolution order would prioritize environment variables first, then check the credentials file where these temporary credentials are stored.\n\nSince `aws sts get-caller-identity` is failing but the CLI apparently works, the credentials in the file might be expired, or the CLI could be using a different authentication path entirely—perhaps through a custom `BEDROCK_BASE_URL` or environment variable that bypasses standard AWS auth.\n\nThe real problem is that Claude Desktop runs as a GUI app with the system environment, so it won't inherit shell-specific environment variables like `BEDROCK_BASE_URL` or `ANTHROPIC_MODEL` unless they're explicitly set in a config file. If the CLI is relying on those env vars for authentication, Desktop wouldn't have access to them. \";\n\t\tString query = String.format(\"\"\"\n\t\t\t\tSELECT DISTINCT * FROM (\n\t\t\t\t  SPARQL\n\t\t\t\t  PREFIX owl: <http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#>\n\t\t\t\t  PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>\n\t\t\t\t  PREFIX rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#>\n\t\t\t\t  SELECT ?o\n\t\t\t\t  WHERE {\n\t\t\t\t    %s {\n\t\t\t\t        ?s a ?o .\n\t\t\t\t        OPTIONAL { ?s rdfs:label ?label . Let me think about this differently.\n\nThe `hadFirstResponse=true` means Desktop sent a message and got a response. Then after ~250 seconds (about 4 minutes), it fails with \"Could not load credentials from any providers.\"\n\nThe pattern is consistent - always ~250-257 seconds. This is very specific timing. Let me think...\n\nThe issue is that ה-Desktop's claude.exe gets credentials at startup. The credentials are STS temporary credentials. After some time, when the claude.exe tries to refresh/rotate the credentials, it fails because:\n1. It needs to call AWS STS to get new credentials\n\n\n2. To do that it needs to go through the proxy with SSL\n3. It can't because the SSL configuration isn't working properly\n\n`NODE_USE_SYSTEM_CA=1` should make Node.js use the system CA store, and the Leumi cert is in the Windows cert store, so that should work. But the claude.exe is being spawned with specific environment variables from `sessionEnv`, and maybe those aren't being passed through correctly or are overriding the system settings.\n\nLooking at the environment setup function, `NODE_USE_SYSTEM_CA` is explicitly set to \"1\", and `GlA()` adds the Windows standard environment variables, plus `cEA()` adds the proxy configuration. So all the pieces should be there. The credentials file exists through the symlink and the config points to the CA bundle, so the initial setup looks right. But `hadFirstResponse=true` suggests it got credentials successfully at first, then failed after about 250 seconds—which points to credential expiration rather than a configuration issue. The AWS SDK normally refreshes credentials hourly, but SSO tokens might have a shorter lifetime.\n\nActually, looking at the timestamps more carefully—the credentials file was modified at 14:40:32 and the failure happened at 14:40:35. So the credentials were replaced right before the error. The claude.exe process had cached the old credentials in memory, but someone swapped out the file, and when it tried to use the stale cached credentials, they were no longer valid.\n\nBut wait, STS credentials shouldn't be revoked just because new ones were issued—they have their own expiration window. And the failure repeats every ~250 seconds, which is way too short for credential expiry since AWS STS credentials typically last at least an hour.\n\nSo maybe it's not credential expiry at all. Maybe after ~250 seconds of inactivity, the session itself times out, and then when claude.exe tries to use its cached credentials again, something goes wrong with the session validation.\n\nNow I'm wondering if the issue is actually in how the credentials are being refreshed. The config file points to a profile with only region and output settings, no credential source specified. And the credentials file is being updated by a background process. When claude.exe tries to refresh its credentials from that file, it might be reading the newly written credentials and then attempting to validate them with AWS STS, but the SSL call to the STS endpoint is failing—though `NODE_USE_SYSTEM_CA=1` should handle that.\n\nActually, let me reconsider the credential chain. With `AWS_PROFILE=ai-devtools-dev` set, the SDK should find the profile in the config file and then look for matching credentials in the credentials file. The credentials file has the access key, secret, and session token, so it shouldn't need to call STS at all—it should just use those static credentials directly for API calls. So why is it saying it can't load credentials from any providers?\n\nWait, I just realized something about the symlink. The credentials file is a symlink to `~/.aws/credentials`, and MSIX apps run in a virtualized filesystem sandbox. Symlinks might not resolve correctly inside that sandbox environment, which could explain why the credential provider can't find the file. But then again, the credentials do load initially at startup, so the symlink must be working at least once. Maybe the issue is specific to how the refresh mechanism handles the symlink, or perhaps the symlink breaks under certain conditions within the sandbox.\n\nLooking at the timestamps more carefully - the credentials file was modified at 14:40:32, Claude started at 14:36:24, and the failure occurred at 14:40:35. That's a 4-minute gap between startup and the credential write, with the failure happening just 3 seconds after the file was updated. This timing strongly suggests a race condition where the credential refresh is happening right as the file is being rewritten, or the old STS credentials expired right at that moment.\n\nSome credential rotation systems revoke old STS tokens when new ones are issued, which would invalidate Claude's cached credentials. The real issue is that Claude caches the credentials on startup rather than re-reading them on each API call. The AWS SDK should theoretically re-read the file when credentials expire, but static file credentials don't have an expiry timestamp, so the SDK might not know to refresh them.\n\nThe better approach is using `credential_process` in the AWS config file, which gets invoked every time credentials are needed and can return fresh credentials with an expiration timestamp that forces periodic refresh.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":"tool_use","stop_sequence":null,"stop_details":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":1,"cache_creation_input_tokens":163,"cache_read_input_tokens":131976,"output_tokens":2885,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":163},"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"}},"type":"assistant","uuid":"a3617b7e-318a-475e-9d51-5a54ff62e1b5","timestamp":"2026-06-02T11:48:45.414Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"eclipse-plugin","cwd":"C:\\dev\\mcp\\mcp-jdbc-server-main","sessionId":"38a741a3-6df6-4733-828e-cce8f5025387","version":"2.1.119","gitBranch":"master"}
